g.data-narration: This diagram explains the structure of agents in AI, covering definitions, types, and real examples.
g.text: The Structure of Agents in AI

g.data-narration: An AI agent perceives its environment through sensors and acts through actuators to achieve goals.
g.text: to achieve goals based on perceptions.

g.data-narration: Agent function maps percept sequences to actions mathematically, while agent program is its implementation.
PEAS framework helps design agents by specifying Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, and Sensors.
Environments can be observable, deterministic, static or dynamic, and discrete or continuous.
Simple reflex agents act only on current percepts using condition-action rules, making them fast but limited.
g.text: Select actions based on CURRENT percept only. Uses condition-action rules.

g.data-narration: Model-based reflex agents maintain internal state to track unobserved aspects using world models.
g.text: Maintain INTERNAL STATE to track unobserved aspects of the world.
Uses model of how world evolves independent of agent's actions

g.data-narration: Goal-based agents consider future consequences using search and planning to find goal-achieving actions.
g.text: Consider FUTURE consequences. Use search and planning to find action sequences

g.data-narration: Utility-based agents use utility functions to measure state desirability and handle conflicting goals.
g.text: Incorporate UTILITY FUNCTION to measure desirability of states. Handle trade-offs

g.data-narration: Learning agents have four components: learning element, performance element, critic, and problem generator.
The vacuum cleaner agent uses simple reflex behavior: if dirty then suck, otherwise move to other room.
g.text: PEAS: Performance - dirt cleaned, time taken
Actuators - motor, suction | Sensors - dirt, location
IF current room is dirty THEN suck dirt
ELSE IF clean THEN move to other room
Responds only to immediate sensory input

g.data-narration: A chess-playing agent uses goal-based planning with minimax algorithm to find optimal moves.
g.text: Actuators - board moves | Sensors - board config
Uses Minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning
Looks ahead multiple moves to find optimal action

g.data-narration: Autonomous vehicles use multi-layered architecture combining reflex, goal-based, and utility-based reasoning.
g.text: PEAS: Performance - safe navigation, speed, fuel efficiency, comfort
Sensors - cameras, LiDAR, radar, GPS | Actuators - steering, brakes

g.data-narration: Remember PEAS framework and agent types for exams: simple to learning agents with increasing complexity.
g.text: Exam Tips: Remember PEAS framework, differentiate agent types, understand environment properties!
